title: The Day I Finally Said No
story:
My first EA job nearly broke me. Not because the work was hard—I can do hard. It broke me because I couldn't say no to anything.

There was this one month where I was working until midnight every night, taking calls on weekends, skipping lunches. My boss would send 'quick requests' at 9pm that would take three hours. And I'd do them. Every time.

I told myself it was dedication. It was being a team player. What it actually was: I was terrified that setting a boundary meant I wasn't good enough.

The moment that changed things was small. My boss asked me to cancel my weekend plans to work on a presentation. Not an emergency—just something that could have been planned better. And for the first time, I said: 'I can have it ready by Monday end of day, but I'm not available this weekend.'

My heart was pounding. I was sure I'd be fired.

She just said: 'Okay, Monday works.'

That was it. No drama. No consequences. She didn't even seem bothered.

I'd spent months destroying my health and relationships to protect a boundary that was never actually threatened. The limit was never about what they would accept—it was about what I was willing to ask for.

Now when I help people with boundaries, I tell them that story. Because the fear is almost always bigger than the reality. And the cost of not having boundaries is always higher than the cost of setting them.
